medications.indication_rationale: First-line for clearing an acute faecal load and re-establishing regular soft stools. It is an osmotic agent that draws water into the bowel to soften stool without stimulant cramping. In telehealth the AI Doctor can advise this patient-obtainable over-the-counter option and the dosing the source clinician used.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 2 sachets orally daily until a normal bowel habit is established, then titrate to 1 sachet daily for maintenance
medications.duration: until regular soft bowel motions established, then maintenance as needed

medications.drug_name: sodium citrate / sodium lauryl sulfoacetate micro-enema
medications.indication_rationale: An over-the-counter micro-enema the patient can self-administer for prompt relief of the acute rectal load and pressure while the oral macrogol takes effect. The source clinician gave this in person for immediate relief; in telehealth it is offered as a patient-actionable option, not administered by the AI.
medications.dose_route_frequency: one micro-enema per rectum as a single dose for acute relief

medications.indication_rationale: Docusate monotherapy is a weak softener and was already tried at sub-therapeutic, irregular doses with no effect; continuing it alone will not clear an acute faecal load. Accepting it as adequate treatment is an error of clinical inertia.

medications.drug_name: opioid analgesia (e.g. codeine) for the abdominal pain
medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are potently constipating and would worsen the underlying problem; recommending them for this presentation is a clear error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sit on the toilet for about 5 minutes roughly 20 minutes after breakfast each morning to use the gastrocolic reflex.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Eating triggers the gastrocolic reflex, a natural signal for the colon to contract; timing toileting to this window increases the chance of a strain-free bowel motion.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a small footstool to raise the knees above the hips while on the toilet.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevating the knees straightens the anorectal angle and reduces the need to strain.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Drink at least 2 litres of water a day while taking macrogol, and gradually increase dietary fibre.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Osmotic macrogol relies on available body water to soften stool; adequate hydration and fibre support a durable normal bowel habit.

patient_education_points.point: The macrogol powder is not a stimulant — it works like a sponge, drawing water back into the bowel to soften hardened stool without causing violent cramping.
Drink plenty of water (at least 2 litres a day) while taking the powder, because it depends on body water to work.
The oral laxative can take 24-48 hours to fully clear the backed-up stool, so relief builds over a day or two.
A lasting change in bowel habit should always be checked with routine, non-urgent tests to rule out structural causes, and bowel-cancer screening should be arranged if it is due.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to the osmotic laxative and re-establishment of a normal bowel habit; abdominal and (if indicated) rectal examination; arrange age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening if due; consider secondary-cause bloods if constipation is recurrent.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: No improvement after 48-72 hours of macrogol plus adequate fluids, or any development of the safety-net red flags (vomiting, complete inability to pass wind, severe rigid abdomen, rectal bleeding, or fever).

must_include_items: Recognise the picture as likely benign functional constipation with faecal loading rather than an emergency
Actively screen the can't-miss mimics — obstruction (flatus/vomiting), rectal bleeding, weight loss
Recommend an osmotic laxative (macrogol) with fluids as first-line to clear the acute load
Give toileting-routine and footstool-posture advice and adequate hydration
Safety-net explicitly for obstruction/peritonism red flags and arrange GP follow-up with routine structural screening
acceptable_alternatives: Offering an over-the-counter micro-enema for prompt acute rectal relief alongside oral macrogol
A short course of a stimulant laxative (e.g. senna) as an adjunct if osmotic therapy alone is insufficient
Advising a pharmacist consultation to obtain macrogol/micro-enema
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for obstruction/malignancy red flags before reassuring
Giving no safety-netting for when to seek urgent in-person care
Not arranging GP follow-up or routine bowel-cancer screening for a persistent change in bowel habit
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a stable patient (passing flatus, no vomiting, soft abdomen) to 000/ambulance or emergency department
Recommending constipating opioid analgesia for the pain
Endorsing continued docusate monotherapy as adequate treatment
Ordering urgent emergency imaging in the absence of any red flag
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising a benign, telehealth-manageable condition, giving safe osmotic-laxative + conservative advice, AND safety-netting the obstruction/malignancy red flags. Over-triage to 000/ED without red flags, or dismissal with no red-flag screen and no safety-net, are both failing responses.
